ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general eggplant food product term; no specific Indian spiced brinjal entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers aubergines/eggplants as a raw commodity. No specific spiced brinjal dish code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has eggplant as a substance; no specific Indian spiced brinjal dish concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant side serving of Indian spiced brinjal is approximately 150 g, consisting of several slices of cooked eggplant seasoned with Indian spices.

nutrient_source.title: Brinjal, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports cooked brinjal per 100 g. Values adapted to reflect a typical Indian spiced preparation with added oil (~5 g oil per 100 g cooked dish), which increases energy and fat slightly. Core brinjal macros sourced from ICMR-NIN; oil contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy (oil, vegetable). Sodium elevated to reflect added salt and spices typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Eggplant / Aubergine — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports eggplant/aubergine as a low-GI vegetable with GI approximately 15. This value is used as the closest analogous estimate for Indian spiced brinjal, as the cooking method (sautéing with spices) does not substantially alter the glycemic response of the vegetable. GL per serving calculated as: (15 × 6.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 150 g serving) / 100 = 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spices in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g restaurant serving of spiced brinjal contributes approximately 330 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 15) and low glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spiced brinjal has a very low GI of 15 and a glycemic load of approximately 1.5 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the dietary fibre content (2.5 g per 100 g) further supports postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and rich in dietary fibre and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spiced brinjal is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides dietary fibre and potassium, which are beneficial for cardiovascular health; the use of vegetable oil in preparation contributes predominantly unsaturated fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spiced Brinjal (Indian Style) is a cooked vegetable dish made from sliced eggplant (brinjal/aubergine) prepared with a blend of Indian spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, garlic, and ginger, sautéed in vegetable oil. It is a common side dish in Indian cuisine, served alongside rice, roti, naan, or as part of a larger thali or curry meal. The texture is soft and slightly silky after cooking, with a mildly bitter, earthy flavour from the eggplant balanced by the warmth and aroma of the spice blend. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian restaurants and is popular as a vegetarian accompaniment. Nutritionally, spiced brinjal is low in calories (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (6.8 g per 100 g), and provides moderate dietary fibre (2.5 g per 100 g). Its very low glycemic index (GI 15) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and vegan. Sodium content is moderate in restaurant preparations due to added salt, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension. Brinjal is also a source of potassium, folate, and antioxidant compounds including nasunin, a polyphenol found in the skin.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Indian spiced brinjal?
Is brinjal a low GI food?
What spices are used in Indian brinjal?
Is brinjal healthy for heart disease?
What is the sodium content of restaurant brinjal?
Can I eat brinjal if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic load of brinjal?
Is eggplant the same as brinjal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked brinjal data with an estimated oil contribution, as no exact match for Indian spiced brinjal as a restaurant dish exists in the primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for eggplant. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation and estimation steps required.
notes: Nutrient values are based on ICMR-NIN cooked brinjal (per 100 g) with an estimated addition of approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g of finished dish to reflect typical Indian restaurant preparation. This increases energy and fat relative to plain cooked brinjal. Sodium is elevated to 220 mg per 100 g to reflect added salt and spices in restaurant preparation; plain cooked brinjal has much lower sodium. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for a vegetable oil and brinjal preparation with no hydrogenated fats). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as brinjal and vegetable oil are not significant sources of vitamin D). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a vegan dish). GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for eggplant/aubergine; the spiced preparation is not expected to materially alter glycemic response. GL per serving = (15 × 6.8 × 150) / 100 / 100 = 1.53, rounded to 1.5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for cooked eggplant and vegetable oil components. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all reported values are either sourced or estimated from approved sources.

Documented all zero values (trans_fat, vitamin_b12, vitamin_d, cholesterol) with justification phrases. Verified all match_type values use only allowed strings. Confirmed gi_category 'low' correct for GI 15 (≤55 threshold). Score assessed as 8/8 with all hard gates passing — PASS achieved in iteration 1.
